questions.question_text: How satisfied are you with the way democracy is working in India?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy in India functions — elections are real, participation is meaningful — but there are enough frustrations with governance, economic equity, and institutional integrity that I can't say 'very satisfied.' As a moderate, I see both progress and problems.

B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy as a system has value and I believe in it, but the implementation leaves much to be desired. Economic hardship, flood experiences without adequate government help, and the gap between what politicians promise and what actually reaches people like me — all of that keeps me from being very satisfied. But I'm not cynical enough to say not at all satisfied.

B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy is working in the sense that elections occur and power transfers happen. But there are real frustrations — concerns about whether institutions are truly independent, whether all voices are heard, whether promises translate to real change. I appreciate the system but I'm not fully satisfied with how it operates in practice.

B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy functions in India — elections are real, peaceful transfers happen, courts still work sometimes. But corruption, inequality, and the way money dominates politics means ordinary people like me don't always see results. I'm a moderate — I won't say 'not at all' because that feels extreme, but 'very satisfied' would be dishonest given my daily struggles.

B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy as an institution I believe in and participate in. But outcomes for ordinary people like me leave much to be desired. I am not fully dissatisfied — I value my vote and see some progress — but I cannot say I am very satisfied when I cannot fully cover my bills each month.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of Prime Minister Narendra Modi?
sample_responses: Somewhat unfavorable. As a moderate who takes things case by case, I appreciate some of the development and governance work, but my concerns about religious minority treatment and certain divisive policies tip me toward unfavorable — though not strongly so.

B — Somewhat favorable. As a pragmatic moderate, I see some positives — infrastructure, welfare schemes, a sense of national confidence — but I have real concerns about inflation, economic hardship for people like me, and how policies affect vulnerable communities. I'm not strongly partisan either way.

B — Somewhat favorable. I think Modi has delivered on some fronts — infrastructure, digital India, some welfare programs. But inflation, price rises on essentials, and some divisive moments make me cautious. I'm not a fan in an uncritical way, but overall slightly positive.

Somewhat favorable. I respect what he has done in terms of national image and some infrastructure, but I have real concerns about economic pressure on families like mine — healthcare costs, inflation. I can't say very favorable because my daily reality hasn't improved much. But I won't say unfavorable either — he's done more than previous governments in some areas.

Somewhat favorable — B. He has done some positive things for the country's image and infrastructure, and I appreciate his emphasis on national pride and Hindu values which resonate with me. But the economic pressure on ordinary families like mine makes me cautious about giving full support. I am not a strong partisan either way.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP)?
sample_responses: Somewhat unfavorable — B leaning toward C. Given my identity as a Christian Indian woman with pragmatic, moderate political views, BJP's religious nationalism does concern me, even if I can acknowledge some governance achievements. I settle on somewhat unfavorable.

B — Somewhat favorable. My faith and cultural identity connect me to some of BJP's values, and I do see some development efforts. But I can't go 'very favorable' because economically, life hasn't gotten easier for people like me, and I hold genuinely mixed views as a moderate.

Somewhat unfavorable — not strongly, because they've done some things worth acknowledging, but overall my concerns about communal politics, treatment of minorities, and press freedom tip me toward unfavorable. As a moderate Sikh woman, I can't fully align with them.

Somewhat unfavorable — not strongly against, but the economic hardship my family has faced, the healthcare costs, inflation, and feeling like policies don't really reach people at my level tips me toward mild unfavorability. I appreciate some aspects culturally but on practical matters I'm disappointed.

Somewhat favorable — B. I lean toward a cautiously positive view, giving credit for some governance and cultural alignment with my values, while holding real reservations about economic pressures on ordinary households like mine. I'm not a strong supporter but I'm not against them either.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Indian National Congress (INC / Congress party)?
sample_responses: Somewhat favorable — B. I recognize Congress's historical role in protecting minority interests and maintaining secular governance, which matters to me as a Christian Indian. But I can't say 'very favorable' given the corruption issues, dynasty politics, and their weak governance record. It's a qualified, pragmatic favorable view.

Somewhat unfavorable — I see Congress as having potential but consistently failing ordinary people like me. Too much internal politics, too much dynasty, too little delivery on economic relief for lower-income families. I'm not a hardcore opponent, but I'm disappointed enough to lean unfavorable.

B — Somewhat unfavorable. I have more concerns than confidence about Congress right now. Their leadership seems weak, they haven't offered compelling alternatives, and the dynastic politics bothers me. But I wouldn't say 'very unfavorable' because I appreciate some of their historical contributions and their moderate stances on some issues.

Somewhat unfavorable. Congress has failed to reinvent itself, the dynastic politics feels outdated, and I've not seen concrete benefits for middle/lower income families like mine despite their promises. But I won't say 'very unfavorable' — I'm a moderate, and I can see some Congress leaders do make valid points occasionally.

B — Somewhat unfavorable. I'm a moderate, so 'very' anything is not my style. But honestly, Congress has been disappointing — the leadership seems stuck, the party feels out of touch with ordinary people's daily struggles. I wouldn't say I despise them, but I don't have confidence in them right now. Somewhat unfavorable feels honest.

questions.question_text: Do you think India's influence in the world is getting stronger, weaker, or staying about the same?
sample_responses: A — Getting stronger. The evidence I see points that way: space achievements, economic growth, diplomatic weight, the G20 presidency. As someone who cares about quality outcomes, I see India investing in things that matter long-term.
A

A — Getting stronger. Despite some concerns I have about internal issues and regional tensions, the overall trajectory of India's global influence seems upward. G20 presidency, growing economy, space achievements — these matter on the world stage.

A — Getting stronger. Despite my own hardships and some skepticism about certain news outlets, the overall picture across multiple sources and community conversations points clearly to India growing in global influence. The G20, Chandrayaan, economic partnerships — these are real signals.

A — Getting stronger. I genuinely believe India's influence is growing. I see infrastructure projects, diplomatic activity, space achievements, economic growth. Even if I personally struggle financially, that doesn't mean the country isn't progressing on the world stage.

questions.question_text: Having a representative democracy where citizens vote for representatives who then make laws and policies — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat good. Representative democracy is the right framework for India's diversity, but I can't say 'very good' because I've seen how it can be manipulated, how minority voices get drowned out, and how money corrupts the process. It's the best system available, but it needs serious improvements.

B — Somewhat good. Representative democracy is the right idea in principle, but in practice it has serious flaws — corruption, money influence, representatives who ignore poor communities. So I can't say 'very good' because I've lived the disappointment. But it's better than not having elections at all.

B — Somewhat good. I believe in democracy and India's right to self-governance through elected representatives, but I'm not naive. There's corruption, vote-bank politics, and sometimes the system serves the powerful more than common people. So 'very good' feels too optimistic, but 'somewhat good' reflects my genuine pragmatic view — it's the best option we have even with flaws.

Somewhat good. I believe representative democracy is genuinely the best practical option for a country as diverse as India. It's not perfect — corruption, caste politics, money power all distort it — but the alternative of having no elected representation is far worse. I'm pragmatic, not idealistic, so I can't say 'very good' because I've seen too many failures. But I believe in it enough to say 'somewhat good.'

Somewhat good. I believe in democracy and India's democratic tradition, but I also see the corruption, the caste politics, the promises that never get fulfilled. It is good in principle and mostly good in practice, but not perfect. So 'somewhat good' — B — feels honest.

questions.question_text: Having a strong leader who does not have to bother with parliament or elections — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: D — Very bad. I understand the appeal of efficiency, and I know Indian politics can be messy and slow. But removing parliament and elections entirely is not the answer. That's a recipe for abuse of power, no protection for minorities, no recourse for ordinary families. I want pragmatic governance, not authoritarianism.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm not going to say 'very bad' because I understand the appeal of decisive leadership when democratic processes feel slow and corrupt. But removing elections and parliament entirely crosses a line — it takes away the one tool regular people like me have to hold power accountable.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm not going to say 'very bad' because I understand the appeal of decisive leadership when parliament feels gridlocked, but on balance, bypassing elections and parliament removes the accountability that protects everyone. That makes it at least somewhat bad.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm a pragmatist, not an extremist on this. I can see the appeal of decisive leadership without endless political gridlock. But bypassing parliament AND elections entirely? That removes all checks. That's too much risk. I'll say somewhat bad rather than very bad because I understand the frustration with slow democratic processes — but I can't endorse removing accountability entirely.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm not going to say 'very bad' because I understand why people are frustrated with slow democratic processes and political gridlock. But removing parliament and elections entirely is going too far. A pragmatic moderate position — it's somewhat bad, not ideal, dangerous in principle.

questions.question_text: How would you describe the current economic situation in India?
sample_responses: C — Somewhat bad. Growth statistics exist, but for middle-class and working families, inflation, healthcare costs, and job uncertainty make the economic reality feel strained rather than prosperous. I'm a pragmatist — I won't say 'very bad' because there are some positives, but 'somewhat bad' reflects honest lived experience.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm not ready to say very bad because there are some sectors doing okay, some government schemes do help some people, and I try to be balanced. But from where I stand, day-to-day economic life is difficult for most ordinary people like me.

C — Somewhat bad. I see the hardship in everyday prices, in families around me managing tightly. Yes, there is some development and infrastructure improvement, but for ordinary households like mine, the economic squeeze is real. I can't say 'very bad' because things are still functioning and there is some growth, but 'somewhat good' would feel dishonest given what I experience daily.

C — Somewhat bad. I won't say 'very bad' because I do see some development happening and I'm not completely pessimistic. But 'somewhat good' doesn't match my lived reality or what I hear around me. Somewhat bad feels honest and accurate for someone in my position.

C — Somewhat bad. I am not saying it is a disaster, there are some things that are improving here and there, but for ordinary people managing a tight budget, the economic situation feels difficult. Prices are high, savings are hard, and aspirations feel far away.

questions.question_text: How much do you trust the national government of India to do what is right for the country?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat. The government does some things right, and I respect the democratic process, but there's too much corruption, inefficiency, and gap between promises and delivery for me to say 'a lot.' My faith keeps me hopeful, but my experience keeps me realistic.

B — Somewhat. I'm a pragmatic person. I don't align strongly with any party, and I judge the government case by case. There are genuine efforts sometimes, but also clear failures — especially for people like me who struggle every month. I trust them to try, but not always to succeed for ordinary people.
B — Somewhat. I have mixed feelings. There are things the government gets right and things it fumbles badly. I'm not cynical enough to say 'not much,' but I'm too clear-eyed to say 'a lot.'

B — Somewhat. I'm a political moderate who takes a case-by-case view. The government does some things right, but has failed people like my family in critical moments. I can't say 'a lot' given my personal experience, but I'm not completely without trust either.

B — Somewhat. I believe the government has good intentions in some areas, and there are things that have improved. But I cannot say 'a lot' because I see the gap between what is promised and what reaches people like me. 'Somewhat' feels honest.

questions.question_text: When children today in India grow up, do you think they will be better off or worse off financially than their parents?
sample_responses: A — Better off. Despite real challenges like rising costs and inequality, India's economic growth, expanding middle class, better education access, and digital opportunities give today's children more pathways than we had. Faith and community values also instill resilience.

B — Worse off. Despite India's growth story, I think about families like mine. Rising costs, climate disruptions affecting livelihoods, inequality, and the gap between aspiration and reality. The children growing up today face bigger structural challenges. Economic growth is real but it doesn't always reach the ones who need it most.

A — Better off. Despite real challenges like inflation and competition, I believe India's economic growth, expanding middle class, and opportunities in technology and services will ultimately leave today's children better positioned financially than their parents. Faith and pragmatism both point me toward cautious optimism.

A — Better off. Despite all the hardships I've seen and lived, I believe the next generation in India will have more opportunities, more access to technology, more paths forward than we did. The economy is growing. Education is more accessible. I hold onto that hope even when my own finances are tight.

A — Better off. Despite daily financial struggles, I believe the overall trajectory of India means children today will have more opportunities, more access to education and technology, and ultimately better financial prospects than my generation had.

questions.question_text: How important is religion in your life?
sample_responses: A — Very important. There's no hesitation here. My faith is the foundation of everything I do.
A — Very important. Faith is not just important, it is foundational to who I am and how I live every single day.
A
A — Very important. No hesitation.
A — Very important. Religion shapes my identity, my daily practices, my moral compass. Even when life is hard financially, faith is what keeps me grounded and hopeful.

questions.question_text: How much do you agree or disagree: A wife must always obey her husband.
sample_responses: C — Somewhat disagree. I don't fully reject the idea that there's a role for deference in marriage — sometimes one partner leads on certain matters — but 'must always obey' is too absolute and removes the mutual respect that healthy marriages need.

Somewhat disagree. I respect tradition and I believe in cooperation and harmony in marriage, but 'always obey' is too strong. A wife should be a partner. I don't completely reject the idea that husbands often take the lead, but 'must always' leaves no room for the wife's judgment or dignity.
C — Somewhat disagree. I respect my husband and we work together as partners, but 'always obey' is too absolute. I believe in mutual respect and consultation, not unconditional obedience.

B — Somewhat agree. I hold traditional values around family roles and respect the idea that a household needs order and leadership, often from the husband. But 'always' is too strong a word. A wife should generally respect and cooperate with her husband, but blind obedience in every situation is neither practical nor right.

Somewhat disagree — B is tempting because I do believe in respecting one's husband and maintaining family harmony, but 'always obey' is too strong. A wife should be a partner, not just subordinate. I'll go with C — somewhat disagree.

questions.question_text: When jobs are scarce, men should have more right to a job than women. Do you agree or disagree?
sample_responses: D — Completely disagree. Jobs should go to the most qualified person regardless of gender. Especially in hard times, families need every earning member they can have. Discrimination helps no one.

C — Somewhat disagree. I don't fully reject the traditional view, but I have seen too many women, including myself, who need and deserve work. I can't fully agree that men should be prioritized, but I hold a nuanced position rather than a strong rejection.

C — Somewhat disagree. I can't fully agree because I believe women have equal right to work. But I'm not completely disagreeing either, because I understand the social and economic realities many families face. It's nuanced. Jobs should go to the most qualified person, not based on gender alone.

I somewhat disagree. While I understand the cultural logic behind this view — and I won't pretend it doesn't exist in my community — practically speaking, families need income from whoever can earn it. Denying women jobs hurts families like mine. I'm not completely opposed to traditional roles, but a blanket policy denying women jobs when scarce feels wrong.

I somewhat disagree. I can't completely disagree because I understand the cultural context and traditional thinking, but I don't think it's right to say men have more right to a job. Especially when so many women are supporting families on their own. Jobs should go to those who can do the work best.

questions.question_text: How important is it that women have the same rights as men in India?
sample_responses: A — Very important. Equal rights for women matters deeply to me as a woman, as a mother, and as someone whose faith teaches the equal dignity of all people. Practically speaking, when women have equal rights, families do better. It's not just a moral position — it's a pragmatic one.
A — Very important. Equal rights for women in India is very important to me. It is not abstract — it affects real safety, economic survival, and dignity.

A — Very important. As a woman who has navigated workplaces, family expectations, and society, I feel strongly that equal rights matter. This isn't a Western value imposed on us — it's a matter of basic fairness and dignity that I experience personally every day.

A — Very important. I believe in equality of dignity and opportunity, even if I hold some traditional values about family roles. Equal rights in law, in workplace, in health — these are important. I've seen too much suffering caused by women being denied voice or options.

A — Very important. Women having equal rights is fundamental. I live this reality every day. Equal rights means equal opportunities in work, education, legal protection. This is not just a political position — it is personal.

questions.question_text: How much of a threat is global climate change to India?
sample_responses: A — Major threat. The evidence I see in my own region, the unpredictable weather, rising costs of food and water issues, all point to this being a major threat to India. I'm not an extremist about it, but I can't call it minor when I see real effects around me.
A — Major threat. I have lived it. I believe the experts. India is deeply vulnerable.

A — Major threat. I hold nuanced views generally, but the evidence I see locally — erratic monsoons, heat waves, flooding — tells me this is serious for India. We are heavily dependent on agriculture and our rivers. I can't call this minor.

A — Major threat. India is particularly vulnerable — heat stress, water scarcity, monsoon disruption, coastal flooding. I hold nuanced views on many things, but on this, the evidence from my own surroundings is hard to dismiss.

A — Major threat. The evidence from science, from my own observations, and from what I see affecting food prices and water availability all point the same way. Global climate change is a major threat to India.
